title: Today's Tech Brief: UCLA seeks pre-litigation resolution with Oracle
summary: UCLA seeks pre-litigation resolution with Oracle over delayed SaaS transformation project; AI ushers in a new era of colonialism

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got a few interesting stories for you today. First up, UCLA is trying to resolve a dispute with Oracle before it ends up in court. Apparently, their software-as-a-service transformation project has been delayed, and they're looking for a pre-litigation resolution. I'm not sure what's more surprising - that UCLA is having issues with a big tech project or that they're trying to avoid a lawsuit. It's like that friend who always says 'let's just talk about this' when you know they're secretly preparing for a fight.

The details are a bit fuzzy, but it seems like UCLA was trying to modernize their systems with Oracle's help. However, things didn't quite go as planned, and now they're facing some challenges. I'm guessing it's not just a simple 'delayed project' situation - there are probably some serious dollars and expectations at play here. I've had my fair share of delayed projects, and I can imagine the headaches that come with it.

UCLA seeks pre-litigation resolution with Oracle is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: software UCLA seeks pre-litigation resolution with Oracle Discussion understood to concern delayed SaaS transformation project Lindsay Clark Lindsay Clark Published fri 29 May 2026 // 16:21 UTC UCLA has entered pre-litigation discussions with Oracle, one of the suppliers underpinning its finance transformation project, which has been delayed by nearly six y... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

AI is ushering in a new era of colonialism - Axios, from Axios, is also worth a quick mention. My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
